questions.question: How does 2026 propose-then-commit differ from the 2023 "Approve?" prompt?
options: It removes the human entirely

The proposal is persisted durably with intent, data lineage, permissions, blast radius, and a rollback plan; committed only on positive ack; verified after — versus a synchronous prompt that gets rubber-stamped
It uses voice instead of buttons
It approves actions automatically below a cost threshold
questions.explanation: The 2023 synchronous prompt produced fast approvals that predicted little and an audit trail the user can't recall. Propose-then-commit moves HITL onto a durable substrate with structured metadata — the shape LangGraph interrupt(), Microsoft RequestInfoEvent, and Cloudflare waitForApproval() all share.

questions.question: What does the idempotency key prevent?
options: Unauthorized users from approving
A retry after transient failure from double-executing an already-approved action — the key ties approval to one unique side effect, making the second execution a no-op
The agent from editing its own proposal
Approvals from expiring too quickly
questions.explanation: Approve "transfer $100," network blips, workflow retries — without the key the transfer runs twice on one approval. It's the same idempotency pattern Stripe and AWS APIs use, reused for agent approvals.

questions.question: What is the challenge-and-response mitigation for rubber-stamp approvals?
options: A CAPTCHA before approving
A checklist requiring positive answers (resource understood? blast radius acceptable? rollback plan?) before the Approve button enables — a forcing function toward escalation or safe-default decline
Two users must both approve
A mandatory 60-second wait timer
questions.explanation: The reviewer who can't tick the boxes asks for clarification or declines. Anthropic's agent-safety research cites checklist-driven HITL specifically as the mitigation for fast, review-free approvals.

questions.question: Which actions warrant propose-then-commit under the 2026 guidance?
options: Every single action including reads
Consequential ones always (irreversible writes, payments, outbound comms, prod DB changes); reversible ones sometimes; reads and inspections never
Only actions over $1,000
Only the first action of a session
questions.explanation: Reads are never gated; reversible local edits sometimes; irreversible/financial/outbound/destructive actions always. Gating reads would train the reviewer to rubber-stamp everything — the opposite of the goal.

questions.question: Why is a post-action verify step required after commit?
options: To log the action for billing
"The commit ran" is not "the side effect happened" — network partitions and races can leave the workflow believing it succeeded while the backend didn't persist; verify re-reads the target
To ask the user to approve again
To measure latency
questions.explanation: Same pattern as DB transactions with RETURNING or GetObject after PutObject. If verify fails, the system is in a known-bad state and alerting engages — silent success is the trap.
